posts.text: every supply shock produces a fork. some countries will lock in more fossil exposure. others won't.
nafta is 31 years old and people are still finding new things it broke
ukraine selling drones to gulf states is a procurement story, not a war story.
models built on 1973 still assume oil is central. some of them are 50 years old and still running policy.
hormuz closure threat and australia ships iron ore through the indian ocean. probably fine.
strait still choked and kharg island hit. that's two nodes at once.
kharg island handles 90% of iran's oil exports and apparently everyone just learned that today.
grid planners building 10-year forecasts right now with oil prices that could be anywhere.
cost of living pressure, rates rising, budget incoming. and still no price on pollution.
a fifth of the world's oil. one narrow waterway.
trump asking south korea and japan to send warships to a strait their energy security depends on. that's one way to ask.
energy prices rising, farmers getting hit. always the farmers.
339 miles of buried transmission line. new york city gets hydro from quebec this spring. that's years of permitting, boring, and grid coordination actually finishing on time.
australia talking about 1bn litres of fuel savings from evs. transmission capacity to charge them is a different conversation.
jerry can rush in australia. supply fine, nerves less so.
offshore wind getting blocked in the us. australia's pipeline keeps moving.
countries cutting back on energy. australia sits on the sun and wind to replace what they're scrambling for.
asia burning more coal because lng got squeezed. australia has the renewable energy to make green hydrogen and ammonia at scale. the customers are right there.
gas companies barely paying resource tax on lng exports and we're watching qatar get hit and prices spike. this is exactly when owning a fair share of the upside would matter.
power prices down 10% because wind and batteries are doing their thing. that's the economics working.
south pars is one of the biggest gas fields on the planet and it just got hit. our lng customers are watching this very closely.
17% of qatar's lng gone for up to five years. japan and korea are going to be scrambling for alternatives. we should be moving faster on every green energy export we've got.
uk poll shows way more voters support net zero than oppose it. the anti-net-zero narrative is just loud, not big. same thing here honestly.

replies.text: zimbabwe's export ban was always more about processing leverage than environmentalism, so the real question is whether sinomine comes back with a refinery deal attached
replies.author: europe-africa.bsky.social

replies.text: the interesting part is whether zimbabwe can hold the line on processing requirements when lithium prices are this depressed and the fiscal pressure is real
replies.author: craigling.bsky.social

replies.text: tariffs on steel and equipment hit mining and processing projects just as hard as they hit the things critical minerals are supposed to go into
replies.author: horadam.bsky.social

replies.text: green ammonia fertiliser from paraguay is an interesting one, local feedstocks, cheap hydro, and a product that basically every neighbour needs
replies.author: eib.org
